coredata.dc:title: Machine learning and DFT dual-guidance of carbon dots implanted SrTiO3 hollow nanosphere for efficient all-pH-value photocatalysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.

coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Lijing
Yang, Tianyi
Wei, Mengjiao
Guan, Renquan
Wei, Wei
Jiang, Jizhou
coredata.dc:description: The photo-Fenton-like reaction holds significant promise for treating low-concentration antibiotic wastewater, yet its progress is hindered by the weak adsorption and activation ability of peroxydisulfate (PDS) and limited pH application range. This work employs density functional theory and machine learning models in tandem to craft optimal photocatalysts for tetracycline (TC) degradation across all pH ranges. Our investigation reveals that carbon dots (CDs) modified SrTiO3 hollow nanospheres exhibit robust PDS adsorption and activation capabilities, facilitating electron transfer from the photocatalyst to SO4 •-. Additionally, the abundant functional groups on CDs confer a protective effect on SrTiO3, shielding it from corrosion by strong acids and bases. Consequently, CDs-SrTiO3 demonstrates excellent photocatalytic performance across the entire pH spectrum, particularly in alkaline and extremely acidic conditions. Furthermore, CDs deposition enhances the solar utilization, specific surface active site amount, and hydrophilicity of SrTiO3. Theoretical calculations and experimental characterizations elucidate the degradation mechanism and pathways of TC, while machine learning models optimize the experimental parameters. This work provides valuable insights into the rational design and adept preparation of high-quality photocatalysts suitable for a wide pH range towards wastewater treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: SrTiO3
Carbon dots
Full pH range
Tetracycline degradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Ball-and-stick model and Bader charge of SO4 •- on CDs (b) and CDs sites of CDs-SrTiO3 (c), (d) ELF images of CDs-SO4 •- and CDs-SrTiO3-SO4 •-.

(a) Schematic diagram of machine learning analysis; (b) Regression curves of XGB and (c) GBDT model based on the prediction and experimental data; (d) Importance of different experimental parameters on photocatalytic efficiency; (e) Correlation matrix heat map of different experimental parameters.
(a) SEM, (b) TEM and (c) HR-TEM of SrTiO3; (d) TEM image of CDs; (e) TEM, (f) HR-TEM and (g) EDS-mapping images of CDs-SrTiO3.

(a) XRD spectra and (b) XPS survey spectra of SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3; (c) Sr 3d, (d) Ti 2p, (e) O 1s and (f) C 1s XPS spectra of SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3; (g) Infrared spectroscopy and (h) contact angles of SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3.

(a) TC degradation efficiency of SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3 without PDS; (b) TC degradation efficiency of CDs-SrTiO3 with different catalyst concentrations; (c) TC degradation efficiency of CDs-SrTiO3 with different PDS amount (pH=7); (d) TC degradation efficiency of 8 wt.% CDs-SrTiO3 under different pH values (with/without PDS); (e) TOC removal rates of SrTiO3 and 8 wt.% CDs-SrTiO3; (f) TC degradation efficiency of 8 wt.% CDs-SrTiO3 with the change of pH value; (g) Free radical trapping experiment of 8 wt.% CDs-SrTiO3 and (h) comparison of the pH range of photocatalytic degradation reported in the literature and this work.

(a) PL spectra of SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3 with the existence of terephthalic acid; (b) ESR h + signal of SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3; (c) UV–vis spectra; (d) Nitrogen adsorption and desorption curves; (e) Transient photocurrent curves, (f) linear cyclic voltamperes (LSV) curves and (g) electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3; (h) ESR ·O2 - and (i) ·OH/SO4 •- signals of CDs-SrTiO3 under different pH values.
(a) The structure of TC, (b) the surface electrostatic potential of TC, (c) the schematic pictures of f +, f - and f 0, (d) the degradation pathway of TC speculated by Fukui function and LC-MS.

Density of states of (a) SrTiO3 and (b) CDs-SrTiO3; (c, d) Ball-and-stick model and differential charge density of CDs-SrTiO3, adsorption configuration and energy of SO4 •- on to CDs sites of CDs (e) and CDs-SrTiO3 (f).
TC degradation performance of CDs, SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3 under different conditions.

head.ce:title: Machine learning and DFT dual-guidance of carbon dots implanted SrTiO3 hollow nanosphere for efficient all-pH-value photocatalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cSchool of Environmental Ecology and Biological Engineering, Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Engineering Process of Ministry of Education, Engineering Research Center of Phosphorus Resources Development and Utilization of Ministry of Education, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan 430205, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The photo-Fenton-like reaction holds significant promise for treating low-concentration antibiotic wastewater, yet its progress is hindered by the weak adsorption and activation ability of peroxydisulfate (PDS) and limited pH application range. This work employs density functional theory and machine learning models in tandem to craft optimal photocatalysts for tetracycline (TC) degradation across all pH ranges. Our investigation reveals that carbon dots (CDs) modified SrTiO3 hollow nanospheres exhibit robust PDS adsorption and activation capabilities, facilitating electron transfer from the photocatalyst to SO4 •-. Additionally, the abundant functional groups on CDs confer a protective effect on SrTiO3, shielding it from corrosion by strong acids and bases. Consequently, CDs-SrTiO3 demonstrates excellent photocatalytic performance across the entire pH spectrum, particularly in alkaline and extremely acidic conditions. Furthermore, CDs deposition enhances the solar utilization, specific surface active site amount, and hydrophilicity of SrTiO3. Theoretical calculations and experimental characterizations elucidate the degradation mechanism and pathways of TC, while machine learning models optimize the experimental parameters. This work provides valuable insights into the rational design and adept preparation of high-quality photocatalysts suitable for a wide pH range towards wastewater treatment.

Density functional theory and machine learning models were synergistically applied to design and fabricate ideal photocatalysts of carbon dots implanted SrTiO3 hollow nanosphere for tetracycline degradation at all pH range.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the escalating environmental pollution caused by antibiotic wastewater has garnered increasing concern. The quest for efficient and low-consumption sewage treatment technologies has emerged as a pivotal approach to addressing these challenges. Among the various methodologies, photocatalytic technology, which leverages sunlight as its energy source, has witnessed rapid advancement over the past four decades [1-5]. Recognized for its environmental friendliness, minimal consumption, potent oxidation capability, and high mineralization rate, photocatalysis stands out as a promising wastewater treatment method capable of effectively eliminating a diverse array of low-concentration organic pollutants from water [6,7]. Furthermore, compared to conventional photocatalytic degradation processes, advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) incorporating potassium peroxydisulfate (PDS) or hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) offer enhanced antibiotic degradation efficiency. However, it is widely acknowledged that AOPs exhibit diminished activity under alkaline conditions, and the limited PDS adsorption and activation capacity of photocatalysts constrains the applicability of this technology in treating antibiotic wastewater [8-10]. Catalysts are the core of photocatalytic reactions, and the current research is primarily focused on developing environmentally friendly photocatalysts that exhibit exceptional performance and possess broad pH responsiveness.

Among the myriad of photocatalysts, perovskite strontium titanate (SrTiO3) stands out for its unique advantages in degrading a variety of low-concentration antibiotics in water, thanks to its abundant raw material reserves, excellent photoelectric properties, and low toxicity [11-15]. However, its widespread adoption in photocatalysis is hindered by challenges such as a wide band gap, high carrier recombination rate, and limited surface active sites. To address these issues, designing a photocatalyst with abundant functional groups is imperative. These groups can confer resistance to acid-base corrosion and enhance the catalyst's activity across a wide pH range [16-19]. Carbon dots (CDs) are effective oxidation activators of PDS, capable of improving the generation of sulfate radicals (SO4 •-) and hydroxyl radicals (·OH) [20-22]. The coupling of CDs with SrTiO3 can significantly enhance the adsorption capacity of PDS and the charge transfer ability from the photocatalyst to SO4 •-. Additionally, the π-π interaction between CDs and tetracycline (TC) can improve electron injection ability and boost the production of ·OH and SO4 •-. This synergy makes CDs-SrTiO3 highly promising for the efficient degradation of antibiotic wastewater across a wide pH range.

Based on the preceding discussion, the present study describes the preparation of carbon dots (CDs) modified SrTiO3 hollow porous nanospheres for efficient degradation of TC in the presence of PDS. The distinctive advantages of CDs-SrTiO3 are outlined as follows: The presence of rich functional groups enables CDs-SrTiO3 to resist corrosion from strong acids and bases, ensuring excellent photocatalytic performance across the entire pH spectrum. The π-π interaction between CDs and TC enhances electron injection ability and increases the production of hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and sulfate radicals (SO4 •-). Efficient PDS adsorption and charge transfer are achieved through the synergistic coupling of CDs and SrTiO3. The deposition of CDs enhances solar utilization, specific surface area, and hydrophilicity of SrTiO3, leading to significantly improved photocatalytic activity of the prepared CDs-SrTiO3 composite. Experimental parameters for photocatalysis are systematically optimized using four machine learning models. The potential degradation pathway is analyzed using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) and Fukui function analysis. The photocatalytic mechanism is investigated through systematic experimental characterizations and density functional theory (DFT) calculations.

Benzoquinone (BQ), ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na), and triethanolamine (TEOA) were of analytical grade and purchased from Adamas. Titanium isopropoxide (C12H28O4Ti, 95 %) was obtained from Macklin. Ethylene glycol (OHCH2CH2OH, 99.8 %), methanol (CH3OH), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99.8 %) were sourced from Tianjin Jindong Tianzheng Fine Chemical Reagent Factory. The deionized water used in the experiments was produced in the analytical laboratory.

Initially, 0.9 mL of tetrabutyl titanate was dissolved in 25 mL of ethylene glycol (referred to as solution A), while 4.9 mmol (1.0080 g) of strontium acetate was dissolved in 25 mL of deionized water (referred to as solution B). Subsequently, solution A was gradually added to solution B dropwise under magnetic agitation. After 30 min of stirring, 0.20 g of NaOH was introduced into the solution, and the mixture was stirred for an additional 30 min before undergoing 30 min of ultrasonic treatment. The resultant mixture was further reacted at 160 °C in a Teflon-lined autoclave for 20 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was subjected to three cycles of washing with deionized water and ethanol, followed by drying at 80 °C overnight. Finally, the obtained photocatalyst underwent calcination in a tubular furnace at 1100 °C for 2 h.

Generally, 60 mg of SrTiO3 and varying amounts of CDs (5, 8, and 10 wt.%) were mixed and stirred for at least 60 min. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 160 °C for 6 h. Among these samples, the one exhibiting the highest activity (8 wt.%) was designated as CDs-SrTiO3 for further characterization.

The efficiency of advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) heavily relies on the charge transfer ability of a photocatalyst to PDS. To delve into this crucial aspect, we employed first principles calculations using density functional theory (DFT) to scrutinize the electronic microstructure of CDs-SrTiO3-SO4 •-. Initially, we depicted the simulated structure of CDs-SrTiO3-SO4 •-, as shown in Fig. 1 (a). A comparative analysis with the structure of CDs-SO4 •- highlighted discernible differences. Bader charge analysis conducted at the CDs sites, as illustrated in Fig. 1(b and c), indicated a more efficient electron transfer from CDs-SrTiO3 to SO4 •- (−0.39 e-). This augmented electron transfer facilitates the rupture of O–O bonds in SO4 •-, thereby fostering the generation of SO4 •- and ·OH radicals. Moreover, the electron localization functions (ELF) of CDs-SO4 •- and CDs-SrTiO3-SO4 •- were calculated and presented in Fig. 1(d). Compared to CDs-SO4 •-, the electrons surrounding SO4 •- exhibited a higher degree of delocalization in CDs-SrTiO3-SO4 •-. This suggests a greater extent of electron transfer from CDs-SrTiO3 to SO4 •-, corroborating the findings from the Bader charge analysis. Given that SO4 •- is derived from PDS, it is evident that the coupling of CDs and SrTiO3 enhances PDS activation and charge transfer from the photocatalyst to PDS, thus establishing the foundation for improved photo-Fenton reaction activity.

Based on the predictions of DFT, we prepared CDs, SrTiO3, and CDs-SrTiO3 photocatalysts for TC degradation (Fig. 2 ). The utilization of machine learning models was employed to enhance the optimization of experimental parameters in photocatalysis, as these parameters have a significant impact on the overall performance. Generally, machine learning is a method wherein machines learn from historical data using statistical algorithms and then use the generated empirical model to guide experiments. Through appropriate machine learning models, optimal experimental parameters can be accurately determined based on a small amount of parallel experimental data.

In this work, four types of machine learning models were used to optimize the experimental parameters. For the CDs-SrTiO3 photocatalyst, key parameters affecting morphology, structure, crystallization, and functional groups include hydrothermal temperature (HT), stirring time (St), CDs amount (CA), and hydrothermal time (Ht). During the photocatalytic experiment, parameters such as pH value, catalyst concentration, revolutions per minute (rpm), and PDS concentration (PC) significantly influence photocatalytic activity. To identify the best matching parameters, 108 control experiments were conducted and given in Table S1 in Supplementary Information, including variations in HT, Ht, St, catalyst concentration (CC), pH value, CA, rpm, and PC.

As shown in Fig. 3 (a), four typical models (GBDT, XGB, KNN, and MLP) were employed to optimize the photocatalytic activity of CDs-SrTiO3. Among these, XGB exhibited the highest R value of 0.92, making it the most suitable model (Fig. 3(b and c) and S1 (in Supplementary Information)). Furthermore, Fig. 3(d) illustrates the influence of each parameter on photocatalytic activity. The impact of HT on photocatalytic activity is evidently significant, primarily attributed to its influence on catalyst nucleation, growth, and crystallization. Notably, the pH value has no significant effect on catalytic activity, suggesting that the catalyst exhibits good pH universality.

Additionally, Fig. 3(e) presents heat maps of various parameters, providing insight into the correlation between different influencing factors. Most parameters appear to be independent of each other. Notably, there are significant correlations between CA and Ht (0.35), as well as between CA and pH (0.22). This suggests that the introduction of CDs directly contributes to the broad pH application range of the photocatalyst. Specifically, the CDs content influences the protective ability of functional groups on the catalyst surface toward SrTiO3.

To further identify the best experimental parameters, twelve sets of artificially designed data were created by adjusting the three most influential parameters (HT, PA, pH) affecting activity. Subsequently, four sets of data with the best-predicted activity by the XGB model were validated through experiments. It was observed that the predicted data aligned with the experimental results. Guided by machine learning, we selected the optimal parameters (listed in Table S2) for subsequent experiments and characterizations. This approach ensures a systematic and efficient optimization process for enhancing photocatalytic performance.

The photocatalysts selected by the optimal machine learning model were systematically prepared and characterized. Initially, the morphology of SrTiO3 was examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As depicted in Fig. 4 (a and b), SrTiO3 exhibits a hollow porous nanosphere morphology with sizes ranging from 50 nm to 300 nm. This hollow porous structure provides abundant surface active sites [23], as confirmed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) in Fig. 4(c). Notably, clear lattice fringes and excellent crystallinity are observed in SrTiO3, with the 0.274 nm lattice fringe corresponding to the (110) crystal face of SrTiO3 [24]. The inset fast Fourier transform (FFT) image in Fig. 4(c) aligns with the reported SrTiO3 structure in the literature [25], further confirming its crystalline nature.

Fig. 4(d) presents TEM images of CDs, revealing their size to be approximately 2–5 nm, with a lattice fringe of 0.32 nm corresponding to the (102) crystal face of CDs. The inset FFT image corroborates these findings [26]. Fig. 4(e) displays the TEM image of CDs-SrTiO3, showcasing minimal changes in the morphology of SrTiO3 after CDs deposition, yet CDs on the surface contribute abundant active sites for photocatalysis, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic activity. Fig. 4(f) provides the HR-TEM image of CDs-SrTiO3, where the lattice fringes of 0.275 and 0.31 nm correspond to the (110) crystal face of SrTiO3 and the (102) crystal face of CDs, respectively [26]. The insert FFT images of CDs and SrTiO3 align with existing literature [25-27]. The close proximity of these lattices facilitates efficient carrier transfer, enhancing carrier separation in the photocatalyst.

Furthermore, Fig. 4(g) and S2 illustrate the EDS-mapping image of CDs-SrTiO3, revealing numerous black spots (representing CDs) on the surface of SrTiO3, with Sr, Ti, O, and C elements uniformly distributed, indicating good dispersion of CDs on the catalyst surface.

Fig. 5 (a) depicts the X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra of CDs-SrTiO3, revealing nine diffraction peaks for SrTiO3 at 22.5°, 32.3°, 39.9°, 46.5°, 52.1°, 57.7°, 67.8°, 72.5°, and 77.5°, corresponding to the (100), (110), (111), (200), (210), (211), (220), (221), and (310) planes of cubic phase SrTiO3 (JCPDS card No. 01–089–4934), respectively [28]. Notably, the position and angle of XRD diffraction peaks for SrTiO3 exhibit minimal change post-deposition of CDs, possibly due to the low content and poor crystallinity of CDs [29]. It should be noted that compared to pure SrTiO3, some weak peaks of SrTiO3 disappear in the XRD pattern of CDs-SrTiO3. To explain the reason for this phenomenon, we consulted the relevant literature, and conducted several XRD tests on SrTiO3, after the repeatability tests we found that they were small noise peaks, which had nothing to do with the introduction of CDs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were utilized to characterize the surface chemical states and elemental species of SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3. The XPS survey spectra in Fig. 5(b) reveal the coexistence of Sr, Ti, O, and C elements in both SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3, with the C content significantly higher in CDs-SrTiO3 compared to SrTiO3. Fig. 5(c) presents the XPS spectra of Sr 3d, where SrTiO3 exhibits characteristic diffraction peaks at 132.64 and 134.36 eV, corresponding to Sr 3d5/2 and Sr 3d3/2 of Sr2+ [30]. Interestingly, in CDs-SrTiO3, the Sr 3d XPS diffraction peak shifts towards higher binding energy, suggesting electron transfer from SrTiO3 to CDs during photocatalysis [31].

Fig. 5(d) displays the Ti 2p XPS diffraction peaks of SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3, with two characteristic peaks observed in SrTiO3 at 458.22 and 464.08 eV, corresponding to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 of Ti4+ [31]. Following the introduction of CDs, additional peaks at 458.74 and 464.82 eV emerge in CDs-SrTiO3, attributed to the Ti-C bond [32]. This suggests that certain carbon atoms from CDs infiltrate the lattice of SrTiO3, establishing Ti-C bonds and indicating a robust interaction between SrTiO3 and CDs.

Fig. 5(e) displays the O 1s XPS diffraction peaks of SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3. SrTiO3 exhibits a diffraction peak at 529.51 eV, corresponding to lattice oxygen species. In addition to this peak, CDs-SrTiO3 reveals two new diffraction peaks at approximately 531.92 and 533.28 eV, indicative of oxygen adsorption and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface, such as hydroxyl groups [33]. The presence of these oxygen-containing functional groups enhances the hydrophilicity of the catalyst, facilitating improved contact between the catalyst, water, and sunlight, thereby enhancing catalyst activity.

Fig. 5(f) illustrates the C 1s spectrum of CDs-SrTiO3, revealing four diffraction peaks at binding energies of 278.85, 284.79, 286.28, and 288.73 eV, corresponding to Ti–C, C=C, C–O, and C=O, respectively. The presence of the Ti-C bond indicates close interaction between SrTiO3 and CDs, serving as an electron transport channel to expedite charge carrier transfer. Additionally, the presence of C–O and C=O bonds confirms the existence of oxygen-containing functional groups, further enhancing catalyst hydrophilicity [34].

To validate this hypothesis, infrared spectroscopy and hydrophilicity of SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3 were characterized. As depicted in Fig. 5(g), SrTiO3 exhibits two characteristic peaks at 1419.2 and 3409.7 cm-1, corresponding to C–OH and O–H bonds, respectively. Furthermore, the peak at 576 cm-1 corresponds to the Ti-O-Ti bond. Compared to SrTiO3, the intensity of peaks in CDs-SrTiO3 is notably weakened, possibly due to C atoms infiltrating the SrTiO3 lattice and forming Ti-C bonds in place of oxygen atoms, resulting in diminished characteristic peaks of SrTiO3 [32]. Furthermore, two additional characteristic peaks at 1528.1 and 1713.4 cm-1 appear in CDs-SrTiO3, corresponding to C=C and C–O bonds of CDs. In comparison to SrTiO3, the intensity of C–OH and O–H bonds is notably enhanced, indicating an increase in oxygen-containing functional groups on the hybrid's surface.

Additionally, as depicted in Fig. 5(h), the contact angle of SrTiO3 is 55.2°, which decreases to 43.2° after CDs deposition. Thus, it can be concluded that the introduction of CDs indeed enhances the hydrophilicity of SrTiO3, thereby laying the groundwork for improved photocatalytic activity [35,36].

The photocatalytic performance of SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3 was evaluated through TC degradation experiments. As presented in Table 1 , SrTiO3 achieved a degradation rate of 42 % for TC within 60 min. However, after the deposition of 8 wt.% CDs onto SrTiO3, the degradation rate significantly increased to 70 %. These results suggest an interaction between CDs and SrTiO3, wherein the deposition of CDs modifies the surface and regulates the functions of SrTiO3, consequently enhancing its photocatalytic activity [37].

Furthermore, upon addition of potassium PDS, the photocatalytic activity of SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3 increased to 55 % and 82 %, respectively. This enhancement can be attributed to the improved oxidation ability of the catalysts in the presence of PDS. Notably, the oxidation performance of PDS is typically heightened under acidic conditions, while it diminishes under alkaline conditions, posing a limitation on the application of advanced oxidation processes in treating alkaline antibiotic wastewater. To address this challenge, the catalyst's activity at different pH values in the presence of PDS was investigated. Remarkably, CDs-SrTiO3 exhibited good pH universality, achieving 90 %, 82 %, and 92 % TC degradation under pH conditions of 1, 7, and 13, respectively, which are much higher than that of SrTiO3.

The photocatalytic performance of SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3 with varying CDs amounts was further evaluated using the optimal experimental parameters. As depicted in Fig. 6 (a), under 300 W Xe lamp irradiation, SrTiO3 achieved a 42 % TC degradation rate within 60 min. Following the deposition of CDs at 5, 8, and 10 wt.%, the degradation rates increased to 60 %, 70 %, and 68 %, respectively. The optimal catalyst with 8 wt.% CDs was designated as the CDs-SrTiO3 sample for further exploration of experimental parameters.

As shown in Fig. 6(b), a catalyst concentration of 0.75 g/L yielded the highest TC degradation rate of 72 %. This optimal concentration ensures effective TC adsorption and an optimal concentration of free radical active species in the solution, thereby enhancing catalytic activity. Conversely, excessively high catalyst concentrations inhibit TC adsorption, thus impeding catalytic activity.

To further enhance the activity of CDs-SrTiO3, varying quantities of PDS were introduced into the photocatalytic reaction system. As depicted in Fig. 6(c), the addition of PDS notably augmented photocatalytic activity, albeit without a linear relationship between activity and PDS quantity. The optimal TC degradation rate of 86.6 % was achieved with 0.125 g/L PDS at the initial pH. However, a further increase in PDS concentration led to a slight decline in photocatalytic activity. This decrease may be attributed to the abundance of PDS acting as recombination centers for carriers, thereby diminishing photocatalytic activity.

To assess the pH application range of the CDs-SrTiO3 catalyst, its photocatalytic activities were evaluated at different pH values. As shown in Fig. 6(d), the CDs-SrTiO3 catalyst exhibited robust photocatalytic activity across the entire pH spectrum. Without PDS addition, its activity was slightly higher under alkaline conditions compared to neutral and acidic conditions, possibly due to the generation of more ·OH and ·O2 -radicals under alkaline conditions by the CDs-SrTiO3 catalyst.

To further boost degradation activity under acidic conditions and broaden the pH applicability range, the degradation activity under different pH conditions after PDS addition was explored. It was observed that activity under acidic conditions significantly improved following PDS addition, attributable to enhanced oxidation ability. This enhancement aligns with the increased activity of AOPs under acidic conditions. However, AOPs exhibit diminished activity under neutral and alkaline conditions, restricting their application in the photocatalytic treatment of wastewater.

It is noteworthy that the activity of the CDs-SrTiO3 catalyst under alkaline conditions surpasses that under acidic conditions, possibly due to a synergistic effect between CDs and PDS. At a pH value of 13, a degradation efficiency of 96.1 % was achieved. The wide pH application range and high photocatalytic efficiency of CDs-SrTiO3 outperform those reported in the literature (Fig. 6(h) and Table S3) [38–50]. This positions it favorably for industrial applications in wastewater treatment across various pH levels.

To further explore the photocatalytic degradation ability of the catalyst, the mineralization rates of SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3 on TC were evaluated. As depicted in Fig. 6(e), the TOC removal rates of SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3 were 40.6 % and 78.2 %, respectively, within 60 min. These results indicate that the introduction of CDs significantly enhances the photocatalytic degradation ability of SrTiO3. As given in Table S3, the TC mineralization rates of CDs-SrTiO3 are also much higher than most of the reported photocatalysts.

In addition to the highly efficient photocatalytic activity, the catalyst also has good photocatalytic stability. As shown in Figs. 6(f) and S3, after the four photocatalytic test cycles (from pH = 9, 7, 1, 13), the photocatalytic efficiency of CDs-SrTiO3 are consistent with the initial value in Fig. 6(d). The position and strength of the XRD peak of the catalyst did not change significantly, indicating that the hybrid catalyst has good structural stability. In contrast, as given in Fig. S4(a), after four different photocatalytic cycles, SrTiO3 also has no significant changes in the position and number of XRD peaks. However, compared to the initial photocatalytic efficiency in Table 1, its performance is significantly decreased at pH = 1 and 13 (Fig. S4(b)). This is mainly due to the strong acid and alkaline environment destroying the surface structure of pure SrTiO3 (Fig. S4(c)), while the structure of SrTiO3 in hybrid remains well due to the protective effect of CDs (Fig. S4(d)). That is, the abundant functional groups on CDs confer a protective effect on SrTiO3, shielding it from corrosion by strong acids and bases, thus the photocatalytic stability of SrTiO3 was greatly improved.

Furthermore, the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate (HER) of the SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3 photocatalysts was measured. As shown in Fig. S5(a), the SrTiO3 photocatalyst exhibited a low HER rate of 800 μmol/h/g. However, after the deposition of CDs, the HER of CDs-SrTiO3 increased to 2304 μmol/h/g when TEOA was used as the hole sacrificial agent, nearly three times higher than that of SrTiO3 alone. Considering the results from the free radical trapping experiment shown in Fig. 6(g), where h + was identified as the main active species, TC could potentially replace traditional hole sacrificial agents during water splitting.

To evaluate the waste-to-hydrogen performance of the SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3 photocatalysts, experiments were conducted without any sacrificial agents. As illustrated in Fig. S5(b), SrTiO3 alone produced only trace amounts of hydrogen due to rapid carrier recombination. However, CDs-SrTiO3 exhibited a much higher HER of 126 μmol/h/g. Remarkably, when TC was added, the efficiency increased significantly to 325 μmol/h/g. As discussed previously, during TC degradation, a large number of holes are consumed, effectively serving as hole sacrificial agents. The aforementioned phenomenon amplifies the activity of photocatalytic hydrogen production and significantly enhances the efficiency of electron-hole pair utilization.

The mechanism underlying the enhancement of photocatalytic activity was systematically explored. Firstly, the influence of introducing CDs on the intensity of ·OH and h + was investigated using photoluminescence (PL) and electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra. Terephthalic acid, which can produce fluorescence at around 430 nm, was employed as a probe molecule to detect ·OH free radicals, thus enabling the assessment of ·OH quantity based on PL spectrum intensity. As depicted in Fig. 7 (a), the intensity of ·OH on CDs-SrTiO3 was significantly higher than on the SrTiO3 photocatalyst after 10, 30, and 60 min of illumination, suggesting that the introduction of CDs effectively increased the quantity of ·OH [51]. Additionally, the h + generation ability of SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3 was measured by ESR spectroscopy. Generally, the ESR peaks are produced by the 2, 2, 6, 6-tetramethylpiperidine oxide (TEMPO) trap agent. When TEMPO is combined with the produced h + after light irradiation, the peak intensity of ESR will be weakened, and the more amount of h +, the weaker the ESR intensity is. As shown in Fig. 7(b), both SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3 showed obvious ESR signal peaks, and the TEMPO-h + signal peaks of CDs-SrTiO3 are obviously weaker than that of SrTiO3, which indicates that under the same conditions, CDs-SrTiO3 can generate a higher amount of h +during the photocatalytic process.

Moreover, as shown in Fig. 7(c and d), the deposition of CDs broadened the sunlight absorption range of SrTiO3, while the smaller size of CDs increased the specific surface area of SrTiO3 from 13.5 m2/g to 25.6 m2/g, providing more active sites for photocatalytic reactions [52]. In addition, the pore diameters of SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3 were given in Fig. S6, both of them present mesoporous structure, and the pore size ratio within 30 nm is larger for CDs-SrTiO3 sample. According to the literature's report, the pore size ranging from 10 to 25 nm is more favorable for the photocatalytic reaction [53].

The carrier transport performance of SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3 was further characterized by transient photocurrent and steady-state fluorescence spectra. Fig. 7(e) demonstrates that both SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3 exhibited obvious photocurrent responses upon light exposure, with the intensity of photocurrent directly reflecting the number of photogenerated carriers. Notably, the photocurrent intensity of CDs-SrTiO3 was approximately twice that of SrTiO3, indicating higher photogenerated carrier abundance and better carrier separation performance [54].

To explore the carrier transport performance under dark conditions, linear cyclic voltammetry and electrochemical impedance curves of SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3 were analyzed. Fig. 7(f) reveals that under the same voltage, the current intensity of CDs-SrTiO3 was significantly higher than that of SrTiO3, indicating superior carrier separation and transfer performance for CDs-SrTiO3 [55]. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 7(g) and Table S4, the electrochemical impedance of SrTiO3 and CDs-SrTiO3 were tested, and the impedance values were fitted by the equivalent circuit inserted in Fig. 7(g). Among them, R s and R t are electrolyte solution resistance and interface charge transfer resistance, while CPE is the constant phase. According to circuit fitting result, the R s and R t values of CDs-SrTiO3 are 21 and 15,082 Ω in the dark state, respectively, which are significantly lower than that of SrTiO3 (62 and 32,859 Ω), indicating lower carrier transport resistance for CDs-SrTiO3 during the photocatalysis [56].

The reasons for the exceptional performance of CDs-SrTiO3 across the pH range were further explored using electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra. As depicted in Fig. 7(h and i), distinct ·O2 - and ·OH/SO4 •- radical signals were observed with CDs-SrTiO3 catalysts under different pH conditions. Due to the stronger trapping ability of 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) to ·OH, the signal intensity of DMPO-·OH adduct was higher than that of DMPO-SO4 •-. Furthermore, it was observed that the SO4 •- radical signal was the highest under acidic conditions (pH=1), while the ·O2 - and ·OH signals of CDs-SrTiO3 under alkaline conditions (pH=13) were much stronger than under neutral and acidic conditions [57-60].

This phenomenon may be attributed to the following reactions occurring under alkaline conditions: O2 + 4OH- → 2H2O + 2·O2 -; h + + OH- → ·OH. Consequently, the CDs-SrTiO3 catalyst exhibited enhanced activity for treating antibiotic wastewater across the entire pH range [61-64].

In order to gain further insights into the photocatalytic degradation mechanism of TC, the surface potential of TC and its susceptibility to attack by electrons, holes, and free radicals were calculated using Fukui functions. Fig. 8 (a) depicts the structure of TC with numbered atoms, while Fig. 8(b) illustrates the calculated electrostatic potential of TC, with red and blue areas representing positive and negative potentials, respectively. The electrostatic potential of TC was observed to be relatively positive and neutral, indicating that it is more susceptible to oxidation by holes and free radicals [65-69].

The concrete data of Fukui functions are provided in Table S5, showcasing three types of parameters: f -(electrophilic index), f + (nucleophilic index), and f 0 (radical attack index), as illustrated in Fig. 8(c-e) through VMD software. Notably, atoms C16, C18, C21, and O19 are inclined towards nucleophilic reactions (f +=0.0194), while atom C2, with a higher f 0 value, exhibits a propensity to react with free radicals. Additionally, atoms C6 and C9 display elevated f +values, indicating their relatively active nature and propensity for nucleophilic reactions. Since no related intermediates were identified, atoms C6 and C9 can be disregarded. Based on the aforementioned inference and the LC-MS results (Fig. S7 and Table S6), we propose two possible degradation pathways of TC (Fig. 8(f)). Initially, O (No.19) of TC (P1, m/z = 444) may undergo attack by h + to yield a carbonyl, forming P2 (m/z = 476). Subsequently, P3 (m/z = 458) is formed through a dehydration process. N24 is then attracted by ·OH/SO4 •-, leading to the elimination of -N-CH3 and -NH2 groups to generate the intermediate product P4 (m/z = 398). P4 undergoes further degradation via two pathways. In pathway I, P4 is driven by h +, leading to a ring-opening reaction at atoms C16 and C21, ultimately resulting in mineralization to generate P5 (m/z = 316). Subsequently, P6 (m/z = 208) is formed through a dehydration process, and P4 may also undergo oxidation to break the C = C bond and form P7 (m/z = 222). In pathway II, P4 undergoes a ring-opening reaction to yield P8 (m/z = 359). Further ring-opening results in P9 (m/z = 337). The amino group on P9 is subsequently removed to form P10 (m/z = 226). Finally, P5, P7, and P10 undergo further degradation to form CO2, H2O, and other substances [70-74].

The bandgap structure of SrTiO3 was investigated through a combination of DFT calculation, UV–vis spectra, and Mott-Schottky curve analysis. As depicted in Fig. 9 (a and b), the calculated state density of SrTiO3 is 2.1 eV, whereas this value is notably reduced to 1.7 eV upon the introduction of CDs. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S8, CDs-SrTiO3 (2.35 eV) exhibits a significantly smaller bandgap compared to SrTiO3 (2.91 eV), which facilitates more efficient utilization of sunlight. As revealed in Fig. S9, the Mott-Schottky curve analysis indicates that the conduction band (CB) of SrTiO3 is approximately −1.4606 V vs. Ag/AgCl, which can be further converted to a normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) of −0.85 V [65,75]. Consequently, the valence band (VB) value of SrTiO3 is determined to be 2.06 V using the equation: E VB = E g + E CB. To further investigate the electron transport direction between SrTiO3 and CDs, the differential charge density of CDs-SrTiO3 was calculated. In the generated cyan and yellow cloud images, the cyan regions represent electron depletion, while the yellow regions indicate electron accumulation. As depicted in Fig. 9(c and d), the electrons are predominantly localized around the CDs, indicating that the interface charges are transferred from SrTiO3 to CDs, consistent with the XPS results [76-79]. Additionally, the adsorption and electron transfer abilities of CDs and CDs-SrTiO3 catalysts for PDS were investigated via DFT calculations. As shown in Fig. 9(e and f), the CDs site of CDs-SrTiO3 exhibits significantly stronger adsorption capacity (E ad ​= −3.3 eV) for SO4 •-​ compared to pure CDs (E ad​ = −2.8 eV).

Combining these findings with the investigation results of photocatalytic active species and intermediates, the possible photocatalytic mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 10 : Under 300 W Xe light irradiation, SrTiO3 absorbs light with energy equal to or greater than its energy band, leading to the generation of photogenerated electrons and holes (Eq. (1)). These charges are then transferred to the surface of the CDs, activating the PDS adsorbed on the CDs-SrTiO3 surface to generate SO4 •-​ (Eq. (2)). Some of the generated SO4 •-​ further react with H2O/OH− to produce ·OH (Eq. (3)). Moreover, another portion of electrons from SrTiO3 combines with oxygen to form ·O2 -​ (Eq. (4)). Under alkaline conditions, O2​ and h + may react with OH− to generate ·O2 - and ·OH (Eqs. (5) and (6)), respectively. Finally, the ·O2 - , ·OH, SO4 •-​, and h + convert TC into degradation products (Eq. (7)) [80-84].
(1) CDs-SrTiO3 → hv (SrTiO3) h + + (SrTiO3) e −/(CDs)e −
(2) S2O8 2− + (CDs) e - → SO4 •- + SO4 2−
(3) SO4 •- + H2O → ·OH + SO4 2− + H+
(4) (SrTiO3) e − + O2 → ·O2 -
(5) O2 + 4OH−→ 2H2O + 2·O2 -
(6)
h + + OH- → ·OH
(7) ·O2 - / ·OH / SO4 •- / h + + TC → Degradation products

In summary, the design of CDs-modified SrTiO3 porous microspheres has led to efficient degradation of TC. The presence of oxygen-containing functional groups on the CDs surface serves to protect the SrTiO3 catalyst from corrosion by acids and alkalis, resulting in robust photocatalytic performance across the entire pH range, particularly under alkaline conditions. Additionally, the π-π interaction between CDs and TC enhances the electron injection and promotes the generation of ·OH/SO4 •-​ species. Furthermore, the incorporation of CDs improves the PDS adsorption and activation capacity of the photocatalyst, while also enhancing the hydrophilicity, specific surface area, and sunlight utilization efficiency of SrTiO3. These advancements enable CDs-SrTiO3 to achieve TC degradation rates exceeding 86 % across the entire pH range, while also demonstrating good photocatalytic stability and structural integrity. Fukui function and LC-MS analysis revealed that TC undergoes nucleophilic and free radical reactions, ultimately leading to its degradation into CO2 and other byproducts. The optimization of experimental parameters through the XGB machine learning model further enhances the efficiency of the photocatalytic degradation of TC. This study provides valuable theoretical and experimental insights to develop advanced oxidation process catalysts capable of operating across the entire pH range.

Lijing Wang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Tianyi Yang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Mengjiao Wei: Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Renquan Guan: Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Wei Wei: Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jizhou Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62004143), the Science and Technology Project of Henan Province (No. 232102240073), and the Key R&D Program of Hubei Province (No. 2022BAA084). Eceshi (www. eceshi. com) was acknowledged for the TEM and XPS analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jmst.2024.08.028.
